summary: Scope whether biv_gaussian structured-RE REML is a tractable unblock or a research problem; produce an adversarially-checked feasibility verdict

verdict.reml_fixes_g8: unproven. No document and no test asserts — even theoretically — that REML moves the biased CENTRE of a STRUCTURED-RE SD at g=8 in the location-scale family. The only banked REML-vs-ML un-shrinkage evidence (tests/testthat/test-reml-bias-simulation.R) is for an ORDINARY random intercept bf(y ~ x + (1|id), sigma ~ 1) at n_id=18, location-only, residual sigma fixed; it shows mean REML SD > mean ML SD and closer to truth=0.8 over 40 reps. That is the wrong cell (ordinary not structured, intercept not phylo field, location-only not location-scale, g=18 not g=8) and it tells you nothing about a phylo/q4 SD. The mechanism by which REML un-biases an SD (accounting for df spent on the marginalised mean fixed effects) is real and demonstrated for the ordinary case, but for the structured location-scale SD the relevant restricted likelihood is a DIFFERENT objective (199:54-60: a scale-side field makes the conditional covariance depend nonlinearly on latent scale values, so it is not the location-only Gaussian restricted likelihood) and has neither been derived in this repo nor simulated. Calling it a fix would be optimism the evidence does not license.
verdict.which_reml: For the g=8 structured location-scale SD bias the relevant object is the q4 Patterson-Thompson restricted-likelihood correction (named in DRM.jl/src/reml_q4.jl), NOT HSquared AI-REML. HSquared AI-REML is the average-information algorithm for an exact-Gaussian location-only sparse mixed model and is explicitly fenced off from q4/scale-axis/non-Gaussian work (178:185-187, 179:3-5,29-32). CRITICAL: I verified that reml_q4.jl and ALL Julia sources are ABSENT from this drmTMB repo (find for *.jl returns nothing). The q4 Patterson-Thompson correction lives in a separate DRM.jl package, so it is not something you can wire up by editing drmTMB R/C++ — it is upstream. drmTMB's own native REML (the Laplace-marginalisation-of-beta_mu route in R/drmTMB.R:817-833) is neither Patterson-Thompson nor AI-REML; for an exact Gaussian it is simply exact restricted ML, and it is location-only by construction.
verdict.smallest_next_step: Build the missing bivariate restricted-likelihood reference, NOT relax the guard first. Concretely: write a hand/lme4-or-equivalent reference for a bivariate Gaussian fixed-mean + phylo-on-mu1/mu2 model and a failing test in tests/testthat/test-reml-bivariate.R that pins the expected REML residual covariance, exactly as the header comment at R/drmTMB.R:1994-1999 demands ('they need a bivariate restricted-likelihood reference to validate'). Only once that reference exists should the blanket abort at R/drmTMB.R:2001 (length(spec$random_names) > 0L) be narrowed to permit mean-side phylo terms while still rejecting sigma-side/q4. The cpp already routes phylo contributions to mu1/mu2 by response index (src/drmTMB.cpp:3163-3171) and builds the q==2 correlated phylo field (3175+), so the marginalisation primitive is present; the gate is the validation reference, not a missing kernel.
verdict.honest_recommendation: Pursue but large, and re-scope. If the GOAL is unblocking bivariate Gaussian structured-RE REML as a validated capability: pursue, because the mechanism is genuinely tractable (R-spec relaxation over an already-assembled cpp bivariate phylo covariance) and only the restricted-likelihood reference is missing. If the GOAL is fixing the g=8 / 0.93-coverage problem via REML: do NOT pursue this as the fix — there is no evidence REML moves that centre, the only relevant REML object (q4 Patterson-Thompson) is in a different package, and the coverage shortfall has not even been shown to be a centre-bias problem REML could touch. The realistic alternative for g=8 coverage is a dedicated NEW simulation (g-sweep on the structured location-scale cell, fitting both ML and q4 Patterson-Thompson REML, comparing SD point bias AND coverage) before claiming any estimator-side fix; widening intervals via t-quantiles is a separate, already-known lever that does not address centre bias.
risks: Conflating estimation and inference: REML's demonstrated effect is on the SD POINT estimate (un-shrinkage), banked only for an ordinary intercept; transferring that to a coverage (inference) claim for a structured SD at g=8 is unsupported by any evidence in-repo.
Wrong-tool risk: HSquared AI-REML is barred from q4/scale-axis (178:185-187); the right object is q4 Patterson-Thompson, which is NOT in this repo, so a drmTMB-only effort cannot deliver it.

No coverage validation exists anywhere for any REML path. The single interval-coverage 'smoke' (test-phase18-interval-coverage-smoke.R) builds coverage tables from hand-supplied estimates via a synthetic helper, not from real fits, so it cannot be cited as coverage evidence. 211 explicitly disclaims calibrated interval coverage (211:56-57).

Univariate structured REML is itself only one validated cell (phylo mean-side intercept, point/bias-direction checked not coverage); scale-side, matched, spatial, animal, relmat, q2/q4 are all rejected (211:23-40, 199:31-46). The bivariate extension is gated on a univariate baseline that does not yet cover the scale side where the g=8 bias lives.

Identifiability/convergence for a bivariate correlated phylo mean field (rho_phylo via tanh(eta_cor_phylo), src/drmTMB.cpp:3175-3176) at small g is untested; the existing penalty/MAP machinery (docs 170/172) suggests boundary behaviour is a live concern that any new validation must report, not assume.

Relaxing the R guard before the restricted-likelihood reference exists would convert an honest rejection into an unvalidated wrong-answer surface — the opposite of the current fenced-off state, which reader 1 correctly notes is not silently broken.

challenge.strongest_objection: REML is not even well-DEFINED for the part of the bivariate distributional model where the g=8 bias lives, so "unblock REML" cannot be the fix for the g=8 problem on the merits — and the verdict's own framing concedes this while still presenting REML as the headline path. drmTMB's native REML (R/drmTMB.R:825-833) is exact restricted ML that marginalises ONLY the mean fixed effects; it is location-only by construction. The g=8 under-coverage the goal cares about is a STRUCTURED location-scale SD problem on the sigma/rho submodels. docs/design/199-native-reml-phylo-asymmetry-gap.md:50-60 states plainly that a scale-side phylo field makes the conditional response covariance depend nonlinearly on latent scale-field values, so "that is not the same restricted likelihood as the location-only Gaussian mixed model" — i.e. the standard Patterson-Thompson correction that un-shrinks an ordinary variance component does not transfer to the sigma1/sigma2/rho12 distributional submodels, and no such restricted objective has been derived in-repo (scope gate rows reml_sigma_side_gate / reml_q2_gate / reml_q4_gate all read "unsupported until ... derived"). The only banked REML un-shrinkage evidence (test-reml-bias-simulation.R) is an ordinary random intercept, location-only, n_id=18 — the wrong cell, exactly as the verdict admits. So transferring an estimation (point-SD) result from the ordinary case to an inference (coverage) claim for a structured location-scale SD at g=8 is unsupported, and the repo's own capstone (docs/dev-log/after-task/2026-06-27-gsweep-certification-and-q4-reframe.md:35-41) attributes the q2 under-coverage fix to the PROFILE channel, noting only that "REML WOULD also fix it" as untested conjecture.
challenge.corrected_next_step: Do not pursue REML as the fix for g=8 coverage. The repo has already executed the higher-leverage path and it succeeded: the g-sweep capstone (docs/dev-log/after-task/2026-06-27-gsweep-certification-and-q4-reframe.md) and interval-reliability rung (docs/dev-log/after-task/2026-06-27-interval-reliability-rung.md) show the slope/sigma/q2/q4-location 'walls' are small-sample artifacts — profile coverage reaches certified-nominal (0.948-0.958, MCSE ~0.01) and q4-location pdHess fragility evaporates (phylo 48.6%→5.0%, relmat 22.9%→0.0%) by g=32, with all 8 cells passing the interval-reliability rung via the profile channel. The realistic next move toward the goal is therefore the named DESIGN/PROMOTION decision (Fisher/Rose) about how near-nominal-at-deployment-g maps to a cell promotion, plus recommending the profile channel and adequate g — NOT engine work. If REML specifically is still wanted as a SEPARATE estimation deliverable, the honest smallest step is NOT to write tests/testthat/test-reml-bivariate.R (it already exists, dated Jun 21, and already pins the fixed-effect OLS/SUR restricted-likelihood reference and already rejects structured means) — it is to DERIVE the structured-mean bivariate restricted likelihood (the piece 199 says is genuinely different and missing) and bank a failing structured-mean reference BEFORE narrowing the R guard at R/drmTMB.R:2001; and even then this touches only mean-side phylo, not the sigma/rho axis where the g=8 bias actually sits. Any g=8 estimator-side claim must come from a dedicated NEW g-sweep on the structured location-scale cell measuring SD point bias AND coverage, since it has not even been shown to be a centre-bias problem REML could touch.
challenge.bottom_line: No — "unblock biv_gaussian structured-RE REML" is not the path to the g=8 goal, and the verdict overclaims by presenting it as a tractable fix while burying the disqualifier in its own risk list. Three things sink the optimism: (a) REML's only demonstrated effect (un-shrinking an SD point estimate) is banked for the WRONG cell (ordinary intercept, location-only, n=18) and there is zero in-repo evidence it moves a structured location-scale SD centre at g=8 — the g=8 shortfall has not even been shown to be a centre-bias problem; (b) the standard REML correction is not well-defined for the sigma/rho submodels where the g=8 bias lives — docs/design/199 explicitly says the scale-side restricted likelihood is a different, underived objective, and the scope-gate TSV fences sigma/q2/q4 REML as unsupported-until-derived; (c) the repo has ALREADY found and validated the real path — the g-sweep capstone and interval-reliability rung show the 'walls' are small-sample and that the PROFILE channel at adequate g delivers certified-nominal coverage, leaving only a design/promotion decision, not REML engineering. The verdict also contains a concrete factual error: it tells you to 'write a failing test in tests/testthat/test-reml-bivariate.R' and calls the bivariate restricted-likelihood reference 'missing,' but that file already exists and already pins the fixed-effect reference — the genuinely missing piece is the STRUCTURED-mean derivation, and that still would not reach the scale axis the goal needs. Pursue the design-signoff of the g=32-certified cells and recommend the profile channel plus larger g; treat REML as a distinct, large, and separately-derived estimation slice, not as the g=8 coverage fix.
